patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] mm: zswap: free cold writeback folios promptly

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/2] mm: zswap: free cold writeback folios promptly

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] mm: zswap: free synchronous-IO writeback folios directly
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Kairui Song suggested renaming swap_cache_alloc_folio to __swap_cache_alloc_folio and updating its kdoc to inform callers that the folio will not be reclaimed if it is allocated off-LRU, and also asked whether adding a bool argument to skip_lru would improve the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] mm: zswap: deferred dropbehind free of writeback folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of writeback folios in the zswap compression system by deferring their free operation to a workqueue that runs after writeback has completed. This ensures that writeback folios are promptly freed even when writeback occurs in an interrupt context, which was previously causing issues with asynchronous block devices and filesystem-backed swap.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the performance of zswap by freeing cold writeback folios promptly after writeback completion, reducing unnecessary reclaim scans and keeping cold memory resident for shorter periods.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Suggested using a bool skip_lru instead of freeing the folio directly, and proposed renaming swap_cache_alloc_folio to __swap_cache_alloc_folio with updated kdoc.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: handle ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item() without aborting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch modifies the handling of ENOMEM errors in the btrfs_insert_dir_item() function, preventing it from abruptly aborting when an out-of-memory condition occurs. Instead, it calls btrfs_orphan_add(), which is also likely to fail due to memory constraints. The change aims to provide a more controlled and recoverable behavior in such situations.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested combining Boris's approach with Qu's suggestion to fix the issue, and offered to implement it in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
Interesting. Between that approach and Qu's suggestion we should be
able fix this up. I'll look at doing that for v2.

Thanks again for the review!

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] btrfs: handle -ENOMEM errors in some synchronous dirops without aborting

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] btrfs: handle -ENOMEM errors in some synchronous dirops without aborting

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] btrfs: split btrfs_insert_delayed_dir_index() into prealloc and commit phases
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the `btrfs_insert_delayed_dir_index()` function into three separate functions: `btrfs_prealloc_delayed_dir_index()`, `btrfs_insert_delayed_dir_index_prealloc()`, and `btrfs_free_delayed_dir_index_prealloc()`. The new functions are designed to handle memory allocation and btree insertion separately, allowing for cleaner error handling and reducing the risk of aborting transactions due to out-of-memory conditions. The original function is refactored into a thin wrapper that calls these new functions. This change enables callers to move fallible memory allocations before the point of no return, making it easier to handle errors without aborting transactions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] btrfs: pre-allocate delayed dir index before btree modification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_insert_dir_item function to pre-allocate delayed dir index resources before modifying the btree. This change prevents ENOMEM failures from leaving the btree in a partially-modified state, and allows for more graceful error handling. The patch also introduces an optional parameter to allow callers to provide their own pre-allocated resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: handle ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item() without aborting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to handle ENOMEM errors more gracefully when inserting directory items. Instead of aborting transactions under memory pressure, it converts newly-created inodes into orphans and returns -ENOMEM to userspace. This change affects create(), mkdir(), mknod(), symlink(), and link() operations. The approach taken involves modifying the btrfs_add_link() function to handle ENOMEM errors and adding orphan handling in btrfs_create_new_inode().

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov questioned whether it's possible for delayed dir index pre-allocation to fail, but btrfs_orphan_add() to succeed due to its own memory allocations, and asked if the issue has been reproduced in practice.
review_comments.raw_body:
I feel like the crux of this series to me is whether you have practical
conditions where the allocation of the delayed_node is failing, but the
allocations involved in btrfs_orphan_add() succeed. It allocates a
btrfs_path and has to walk the btree which might have to read the node
at every level which might need to allocate 16k extent buffers and
extent buffer objects and xarray storage for each one. For size
reference, on my build (maybe debug..?) a delayed_node is 552 bytes,
while a btrfs_path is 112 and an extent_buffer is 432. So they are
pretty similar in size (not to mention the 16k of node file backed
memory we are sort of likely to have to allocate if we are under
reclaim)

Were you able to reproduce this issue and help in practice or is this a
theoretical / structural improvement?

With that said, all the prealloc wiring looks good to me in general, and
it seems to be a pretty clean win for the "name exists" case in the next
patch.

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the patch's effectiveness in preventing filesystem aborts under heavy memory pressure, acknowledging it's difficult to reproduce but noting there are existing records of such incidents and proposing to use ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls to make testing easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
I didn't really try to reproduce this in earnest. We only see it in our
fleet under heavy memory pressure, and even then at such low frequency,
I doubt our chances of hitting this on anything other than a huge set
of machines.

So, theoretical / structural, but we have record of filesystem aborts
where the stack indicates that this would have prevented it. Userland
would have gotten an -ENOMEM back but the fs wouldn't have aborted.

I see that there are some ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls in btrfs. We
could wire some of these functions up with that, which would make this
easier to test. I'll look into that in the meantime.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov expressed concern that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link() may not be effective, as it could still lead to an abort when calling btrfs_orphan_add(), and requested concrete evidence of its benefits.
review_comments.raw_body:
My concern is not that we don't hit ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link(), since
like you said we can observe that in abort logs. I am worried that even
if we try to handle it gracefully, we will just ENOMEM in
btrfs_orphan_add() and abort anyway. That is why I was wanting to see
some more concrete evidence this actually helps to make it worth the
complexity.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a potential issue where objects are allocated successfully but then fail to be added as orphans due to an error in btrfs_orphan_add(), and suggested preallocating the main allocation (btrfs_alloc_path?) in the transaction handle.
review_comments.raw_body:
In the case where we handle this gracefully, we won't hit that because
it will have returned -ENOMEM before that point. But, you do have a
good point that we could allocate these objects successfully, and then
hit an error in btrfs_orphan_add() anyway.

One thought: It looks like the main allocation in that codepath is
btrfs_alloc_path()? We could consider preallocating that too -- maybe
stash it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle?

Thanks! This is good food for thought.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov noted that when handling ENOMEM from btrfs_insert_dir_item(), the patch calls btrfs_orphan_add() which itself may fail and return ENOMEM, potentially leading to an infinite loop of errors.

Reviewer Boris Burkov expressed confusion about the patch's handling of ENOMEM in btrfs_insert_dir_item(), specifically asking for clarification on the inline comments he made earlier.

Reviewer pointed out that the patch description and code changes do not match, as the patch description mentions delayed dir index pre-allocation but the code does not implement it.
review_comments.raw_body:
And when we get ENOMEM, we call btrfs_orphan_add() isntead of
unilaterally aborting. I contend that this call is now likely to ENOMEM

---

I am still a bit confused. I made comments inline closer to the the code.
Sorry if I am being dense or missing the point!

---

Take a peek at https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/cover.1782249000.git.boris@bur.io/

which I need to RESEND :)

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested using on-stack memory for btrfs_alloc_path() to avoid potential trans aborts due to ENOMEM, citing a similar practice in btrfs-progs and the relatively small size of the structure (112 bytes).
review_comments.raw_body:
For btrfs_alloc_path(), you can just use on-stack memory for that.

That's the practice we utilize for btrfs-progs, and it's still 
acceptable for kernel, since that structure is only 112 bytes.

We should not switch all btrfs_path to on-stack ones, but for critical 
ones that memory allocation can lead to trans abort, I'd say it's 
definitely worth the extra on-stack memory usage.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a need to restructure the code in response to reviewer feedback, agreeing to make changes in v2.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for further work
review_comments.raw_body:
Interesting. Between that approach and Qu's suggestion we should be
able fix this up. I'll look at doing that for v2.

Thanks again for the review!

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] btrfs: pre-allocate delayed dir index for non-overwrite rename
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a feature to the Btrfs file system that pre-allocates delayed directory index resources before performing non-overwrite renames. This ensures that ENOMEM errors are returned early, preventing potential data corruption. The patch introduces a new function `btrfs_prealloc_delayed_dir_index` and modifies existing functions such as `btrfs_add_link` to handle pre-allocated resources. The goal is to improve the robustness of Btrfs by catching memory allocation failures before making irreversible changes to the file system's internal state.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a concern about handling ENOMEM errors in btrfs_insert_dir_item() without causing an abort, specifically focusing on whether this approach will lead to further ENOMEM errors in other functions like btrfs_orphan_add(). The patch aims to provide more concrete evidence that this handling strategy is effective and worth the added complexity.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Boris Burkov questioned whether the patch addresses a real-world issue, citing concerns that allocations involved in btrfs_orphan_add() might succeed while those for delayed_node fail due to their similar size and potential memory requirements under reclaim conditions.
review_comments.raw_body:
I feel like the crux of this series to me is whether you have practical
conditions where the allocation of the delayed_node is failing, but the
allocations involved in btrfs_orphan_add() succeed. It allocates a
btrfs_path and has to walk the btree which might have to read the node
at every level which might need to allocate 16k extent buffers and
extent buffer objects and xarray storage for each one. For size
reference, on my build (maybe debug..?) a delayed_node is 552 bytes,
while a btrfs_path is 112 and an extent_buffer is 432. So they are
pretty similar in size (not to mention the 16k of node file backed
memory we are sort of likely to have to allocate if we are under
reclaim)

Were you able to reproduce this issue and help in practice or is this a
theoretical / structural improvement?

With that said, all the prealloc wiring looks good to me in general, and
it seems to be a pretty clean win for the "name exists" case in the next
patch.

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jeff Layton expressed concerns about the practicality of reproducing the ENOMEM issue in btrfs_insert_dir_item() under heavy memory pressure, but noted that there are records of filesystem aborts where this patch would have prevented them. He suggested using ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() to make testing easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
I didn't really try to reproduce this in earnest. We only see it in our
fleet under heavy memory pressure, and even then at such low frequency,
I doubt our chances of hitting this on anything other than a huge set
of machines.

So, theoretical / structural, but we have record of filesystem aborts

I see that there are some ALLOW_ERROR_INJECTION() calls in btrfs. We
could wire some of these functions up with that, which would make this

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link() does not necessarily prevent aborts in btrfs_orphan_add(), and requests more concrete evidence of the patch's benefits.
review_comments.raw_body:
My concern is not that we don't hit ENOMEM in btrfs_add_link(), since
like you said we can observe that in abort logs. I am worried that even
if we try to handle it gracefully, we will just ENOMEM in

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jeff Layton expressed concern that even if ENOMEM is handled gracefully, the patch may still result in an abort due to subsequent errors in btrfs_orphan_add(), and suggested preallocating the main allocation in that codepath by stashing it in a new pointer in btrfs_trans_handle().
review_comments.raw_body:
In the case where we handle this gracefully, we won't hit that because
it will have returned -ENOMEM before that point. But, you do have a
good point that we could allocate these objects successfully, and then
hit an error in btrfs_orphan_add() anyway.

One thought: It looks like the main allocation in that codepath is

Thanks! This is good food for thought.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Jeff Layton's concern about the patch handling ENOMEM in btrfs_orphan_add() by explaining that when ENOMEM occurs, it will instead call btrfs_orphan_add(), which is likely to also fail with ENOMEM.

Author expressed confusion about reviewer's concern, stating they had provided inline comments near the relevant code and apologizing for any misunderstanding.

Author acknowledges reviewer's concern that handling ENOMEM in btrfs_insert_dir_item() might not be sufficient, as it could still lead to an abort in btrfs_orphan_add(). Author promises to resend evidence to support the complexity of the patch.
